contents: Systematic review and individual patient data analysis of pediatrichead and neck squamous cell carcinoma: An analysis of 217 casesV. Bhanu Prasad a, Supriya Mallick a, *, Ashish Dutt Upadhyay b, G.K. Rath aa Department of Radiation Oncology, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, Indiab Department of Bio-Statistics, All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, Indiaa r t i c l e i n f o

Article history:Received 3 September 2016Received in revised form4 November 2016Accepted 7 November 2016Available online 12 November 2016Keywords:PediatricHead and neckSquamous cell carcinomaRadiotherapya b s t r a c tIntroduction: Pediatric head and neck Squamous cell carcinoma (PHNSCC) is a rare disease. The optimumtreatment and outcome remains poorly understood because of rarity.Methods: We conducted an individual patient data analysis of PHNSCC. Two authors independentlysearched PubMed, google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies using following search words:Pediatric Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC till June 1, 2016 published in Englishlanguage.Results: Total of 217 patients of PHNSCC were found in the literature. Median age among the cohort was15 years (Range: 0e20 years) with a clear male preponderance. Oral cavity tumors were commonest 75(70%) followed by laryngeal neoplasms 16(15%). Median disease free survival was 9 months (Range: 0e216 months). Median overall survival was 48 months (Range: 1e216 months). In univariate analysistreatment modality had significant impact on disease free survival (DFS). Whereas, patients treated withSurgery, Laryngeal primary had significantly better OS. Patients with associated fanconis anemia hadsignificantly worse overall survival (OS).Conclusion: PHNSCC is a rare disease with poorer outcome. Associated DNA defects leads to poorer OS.Patients treated with surgery alone or surgery followed by adjuvant radiation had better DFS and OS.Molecular profiling and personalized therapy may improve survival with limited toxicity.(c) 2016 Elsevier Ireland Ltd. All rights reserved.

1. IntroductionSquamous cell carcinoma of head and neck (HNSCC) is consid-ered disease of sixth or seventh decade of life. These tumors aremost commonly associated with tobacco, alcohol consumption andill-fitting dentures. HNSCC is rare entity in pediatric age group (20years as defined by the American college of Pediatrics) [1e3]. Riskfactors in Pediatric age group are unique including DNA repairdefects like Fanconis anemia, Blooms syndrome, Xeroderma pig-menstosum, dyskeratosis congenita, connexin mutations, chronicgraft-versus-host disease, epidermolysis bullosa, human papillo-mavirus infection(HPV), immunosuppressive states like organtransplant[4e10].Becauseofraritylittleagreementexistsregarding optimum treatment options for these patients and oftenprotocols for adult patients are advocated. But, younger age,anticipated morbidity and impact of associated risk factors make itdifficult to formulate optimum treatment and outcome. SEERdatabase analysis of 54 pediatric HNSCC (PHNSCC) reported supe-rior outcomes in children compared to adults [11]. However, fewother reports have reported dismal outcomes as well [12]. Weintended to perform this systematic review and individual patientdata analysis of published literature to assess the demographicprofile, pattern of care and assess survival outcomes.2. Materials and methods2.1. Date source and search methodTwo authors (BP and SM) independently searched PubMed,google search, and Cochrane library for eligible studies usingfollowing search words: pediatric Head and neck squamous cellcarcinoma, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma under age of* Corresponding author.E-mail addresses: (V. Bhanu Prasad), (S. Mallick), (A.D. Upadhyay), (G.K. Rath).Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

journal homepage:  2016 Elsevier Ireland Ltd. All rights reserved.20, Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma in young, PHNSCC tillJune 1, 2016 published in English language. The details of searchstrategy in PubMed have been mentioned in supplementary digitalcontent. References from the primary search result also weremanually searched for potentially eligible studies.2.2. Study selectionWe included only published articles describing HNSCC andtreatment 20 years of age, fully published case reports or caseseries. Articles on pathology and genetics alone without report ontreatment and clinical outcomes were excluded from the analysis.Two independent authors (BP) and(SM) selected the eligiblestudies. Any disagreement between two authors was solved bydiscussing with a third author (GKR). Study selection has beendepicted in Fig. 1 PRISMA flow diagram.2.3. Data collection and data itemsTwo authors independently (BP, SM) extracted all data from theeligible trials. The following data were collected from each of thestudy: name of the first author, year of publication, sex, age, site ofdisease, TNM staging, and treatment received, associated disease,disease free survival (DFS), Overall survival (OS). Initially, all datawere tabulated in Microsoft Excel spread sheet. We neither askedthe author(s) nor contacted the publishers for any unpublisheddata. Pooled statistical analyses were performed by ADU.

2.4. Statistical analysisData was analysed and categorical variables were summarizedby frequency (%) and quantitative variables were summarized bymedian and range. Survival outcomes were calculated from thetime of diagnosis. DFS was calculated from the time of treatment tolocal progression. Univariate analysis (log rank test) was used tofind impact of different prognostic variables on DFS and OS, and pvalue of 0.05 was taken as significant. Kaplan Meier method wasused for survival analysis. STATA 12 software was used for all sta-tistical analysis.3. Results3.1. Patient characteristicsTotal of 217 patients of PHNSCC were found in the literature.Individual patient data was available for 107 patients from 54publications and statistical analysis was performed on 107 patients.Median age of the cohort was 15 years (Range: 0e20 years).22(20.6%) patients were below 10 years and 85(79.4%) were be-tween 11 and 20 years of age. Sex ratio was skewed in favor of maleswith ratio of 1.7:1 (Male: 66, female: 38). Oral cavity tumors werecommonest 75 (70%) followed by laryngeal neoplasms 16(15%)[Fig. 2]. Among oral cavity tumors, tongue-56(74.67%) was involvedmost commonly followed by gingiva 10(13.3%), retromolar trig-one1(1.3%), Upper lip 1(1.3%), Lower lip 1(1.3%), buccal mucosa4(5.3%), hard palate 1(1.3%), floor of mouth 1(1.3%) [Fig. 3].11(10.2%) patients had DNA repair defect, fanconis anemia and9(8.4%) patients had other risk factors like smoking(3), tobaccochewing(1), HPV infection [1], xerodera pigmenstosa(2),retino-blastoma survior(1) and sharp teeth(1).3.2. Pattern of careSurgery alone was used in 42(39.3%), radiotherapy alone in15(14%), surgery followed by adjuvant radiotherapy in 24(22.4%),concurrentradiotherapyandchemotherapyin15(14%),tri-modality therapy 3(2.1%). Treatment data was not available for 8(7.5%) [Table 2]. Fig. 4 depicts the various treatment modalitiesused.

Out of 15 patients treated with radiation only external beamradiotherapy was used in 11 patients and brachytherapy was usedin 4 patients. Interstitial brachytherapy with radon seeds andexternal beam was used in 2 patients, 10 g of potassium Iodide wasused in 1 patient, and interstitial brachytherapy with neck dissec-tion was used in 1 patient. Surgery with or without radiotherapywas used in 66 patients. Out of these 66 patients surgery for theprimary site alone was done in 31 patients and surgery with neckdissection was done in 20 patients. Among 20 neck dissections 8were selective neck dissections, 9 were modified neck dissection, 1radical neck dissection and in 2 patient's neck dissection type werenot specified. Brachytherapy for primary with neck dissection fornodes was used in 1, brachytherapy followed by wide local excisionand neck dissection was done in 2 patients. Neoadjuvant chemo-therapy followed by wide local excision and modified neckdissection followed by post-operative radiotherapy was used in 1patient. Surgery followed by post-operative radiotherapy was usedin 11 patients. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy was used in 4 patientsamong which 1 patient had docetaxel, cisplatin and 5 Fluorouraciland 1 patient received paclitaxel and carboplatin and data was NAin 2 patients. Brachytherapy with chemotherapy was used in 1patient and WLE with adjuvant chemo-radiation was used in 1patient. Palliative chemotherapy alone was used in 6 patients withHydroxyurea-1,Methotrexate-1,Methotrexatewithcyclophosphamide-1, Retinoic acid and 5 Flououracil-1, data notavailable-2 patients.3.3. Survival outcomeMedian disease free survival was 9 months (Range: 0e216

months). 2 and 5 year DFS for oral cavity and non-oral cavity pri-mary was 75.7%, 75.7% and 65.3%, 65.3% respectively. However, thedifference was not statistically significant (p  1/4 0.31). 5 year DFS was87.2% for patients treated with surgery only and 67.1% for patientstreated with surgery followed by adjuvant radiation. Whereas5year DFS was 28.5%, for patients treated with radiation alonep  1/4 0.0013. 5 year DFS for stage 1, 2, 3 were 86.6%, 78.5% and 40.8%FactorDFSOS2year DFS95% CIp value2year OS95% CIp valueP values that were significant are given in bold.

Oral CavityOropharynxHypopharynxMandibleLarynxMaxillarespectively; p  1/4 0.11. 2 and 5 year DFS for the entire cohort was72.3% [95% CI: 0.59e0.81)] and 72.3% [95% CI: 0.59e0.81)]. Age,time of publication, sex, associated syndromes had no significantimpact on DFS.Median overall survival was 48 months (Range: 1e216 months).2 and 5 year OS was 57.5% [95%CI: 0.44e0.68] and 44.8% [95% CI:0.28e0.59] respectively. 2 year OS for patients treated with surgeryalone, or surgery followed by adjuvant radiation, radiotherapyalone was 49.6%, 68.6% and 22.2% respectively. 5 year OS was 72.7%for patients 10 years of age compared to 54.4% for >10 years ofage, but p value was not significant. Early stage patients had asignificantly better survival compared to patients in advanced stageof disease (p  1/4 0.0097). Patients treated with Surgery alone andsurgery followed by adjuvant radiation had 5 year OS of 66.2% and68.6% respectively. The impact of treatment modality was signifi-cantly favouring surgery compared to radiotherapy alone andradio-chemotherapy, p  1/4 0.004. 2 year OS was significantly worsefor patients with associated Fanconis anemia, (p  1/4 0.00001). Pa-tients treated earlier than year 2000 had a significantly bettersurvival compared to those treated after 2000 (p  1/4 0.025). How-ever, there was no impact of sex, oral cavity vs. non-oral cavitytumor on OS. The impact of different prognostic variables on DFSand OS has been tabulated in Table 1. Fig. 5 depicts Kaplan Meiercurves for DFS of the entire cohort and impact of variables on DFS.Fig. 6 depicts Kaplan Meier curves for OS of the entire cohort andimpact of variables on OS.

3.4. Pattern of failureRecurrence was documented in 25(23.4%) patients. Local re-currences was most frequent in 17(68%), followed by local withsimultaneous systemic failure in 2(8%), systemic alone (lungmetastasis) in 6 (24%) patients. Recurrence was found in 11.1%,16.6% and 33.3% patients of Stage I, stage II and Stage III respec-tively. 10% patients treated with surgery alone experienced recur-rence, whereas 22.73% treated with surgery and adjuvant radiationhad recurrence. 36.3% patients treated with radiation alone and38.5% patients treated with chemo-radiotherapy had recurrence.4. DiscussionThe present analysis is aimed to look into the demography,pattern of care and survival outcome of pediatric patients withHNSCC. PHNSCC is a rarity as shown by only 217 cases over acentury of literature search. SEER database analysis by Morris et al.reported female predisposition (37.0% vs. 31.7%, p  1/4 0.04 [13]. But inthe present study 66(63.46%) patients were male and 38 (36.54%)were female with a male preponderance. In contrast to adultHNSCC, nearly 81.34% patient had no documented risk factors. Risk

factors were unique and ranged from DNA repair defects, the mostcommon followed by fanconis anemia [14]. Though uncommon,early onset of tobacco chewing can be risk factor for PHNSCC aswell, particularly in the developing countries [15]. 79.4% patientsbelong to the age group between 10 and 20 years compared to firstdecade of life. This finding further supports the hypothesis ofdelayed development of malignancy in patients with DNA repairdefects. In an earlier report, out of 30 PHNSCC patients with sys-temic disease 21 had FA,4 had XP and 26 patients were in the agegroup of 10e20 years [16]. Oral cavity tumors were most commonseen in 70%, with tongue being the most common affecting 64children followed by gingiva. Tongue and gingiva has very highniche of stem cells which may be responsible for increased carci-nogenesis from these subsites [17].PHNSCC have a tendency topresent in advanced stage with delay in diagnosis due to lack ofclinical suspicion and consideration of benign pathologies as dif-ferentials. Surgery was the most common treatment modality usedin 40%.Surgery alone has been most commonly used since most ofthe patients had oral cavity tumor and most of them were in earlystage of presentation. 2 year DFS and OS were best in surgery armsince 26(61%) of patients in surgery alone had stage 1 and stage 2disease. Similar results were reported in the SEER analysis byMorris et al. as well [13]. In univariate analysis patients treated withsurgery had significantly better DFS, OS compared to patientstreated with radiation alone or surgery followed by adjuvant ra-diation. Outcomes in radiotherapy alone were dismal since intent inmost cases was palliative only. 14% patients received concurrentchemo-radiation and 3% received tri-modality therapy. Brachy-therapy has been used sparingly with no clear evidence to advocateusage in pediatric patients. 5 year DFS for stage 1, 2, 3 were 86.6%,78.5% and 40.8% respectively. But, DFS for patients with stage 4 was76.15% [95%CI (0.43e0.92)] similar to stage 2 which may be due toselective reporting and publication bias. These stage wise results,though not for separate subset of disease, looks no different fromtheadultcounterparts.Weintendedtolookintopossible

prognostic factors. However, other than treatment modality noneof the prognostic factors showed a significant impact on DFS. In thepresent analysis, patients with Fanconis anemia had significantlyworse OS compared to patients with no known risk factors. Patientsbelow 10 years of age, year of publication <2000, stage of disease,associated syndromes, and type of treatment modality employedhad a significant impact on OS on univariate analysis.This is the first systematic review of PHNSCC that has attemptedto prognosticate patients and assess survival outcomes. The limi-tations of this systematic review are varied heterogeneity amongstudies reported, underreporting of data in various articles, widevariation in treatment modalities across the century and absence oftoxicity reporting. In addition, these reports followed contempo-rary staging which makes it difficult to interpret and pool the data.Point should be made that enthusiasm of reporting such rare tu-mors leave behind the opportunity to report all important pa-rameters. However, being a rare disease it is not possible to havelarge data and well described treatment options. This analysis willprovide care givers an insight and a real world scenario to formu-late optimum treatment option for PHNSCC patient and accumulatedata for future analysis.5. ConclusionPHNSCC is extremely rare entity with distinct challenges to thephysician and care givers. The treatment principles employedacross institutes are similar to adults with outcomes comparable toadult counterparts. Surgery was the primary modality with bestoutcomes, as mostly early stage patients are offered such treat-ment. PHNSCC with systematic association has poor outcomes withdismal prognosis. Further molecular analysis of tissue specimensmay throw some light on the exact etio-pathogenesis of this raresubset.DisclosuresThe authors have nothing to disclose.Meeting presentationNot presented.Financial support
